planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in partial shade to full sun in spring; Korean feather reed grass is one of the very few ornamental grasses that performs acceptably in partial shade — a significant advantage in gardens where most grasses would fail to flower or develop properly.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, average soil in partial shade to full sun; Korean feather reed grass is a late-flowering Asian ornamental grass that sets it apart from most others by producing its dense, bottlebrush-like, pinkish-silver flower panicles in late summer and early autumn (August–October) rather than in early summer — arriving as many spring-blooming grasses have already set seed and begun to decline. The fluffy, initially pink-tinted, feathery panicles age to a warm, creamy-tan that persists through winter, providing months of multi-season interest on the upright but gracefully arching stems. Importantly, it is one of the very few ornamental grasses that performs in partial shade — maintaining reasonable flowering and attractive foliage in dappled light where most grasses would etiolate and fail to flower. This shade tolerance makes it particularly valuable for partially shaded borders and woodland edges where ornamental grasses are typically unusable. Compact, clump-forming, and non-invasive. Excellent combined with hostas and late asters in partially shaded autumn borders.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in well-drained, average to poor, slightly acidic to alkaline soil in full sun in spring; ravenna grass thrives in hot, dry, poor conditions and actually performs better in infertile soil — rich, fertile soil produces excessive, floppy growth.

directions: Plant divisions in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun; ravenna grass (Plume grass, Hardy pampas grass, Erianthus) is the cold-hardy alternative to pampas grass for northern zone 6–9 gardens — an enormous, dramatic, clump-forming grass that rises to 8–14 feet with wide, arching, blue-gray-green leaves developing attractive silvery and purplish tones in autumn, and produces enormous, feathery, silvery-white, pampas-like flower plumes in late summer that arch dramatically from the top of the plant and dry to a warm, creamy-tan for winter. The scale is truly impressive — a single established clump rivals the visual impact of a small tree and provides a genuine architectural focal point in the large-scale landscape. Hardy to zone 6 (unlike true pampas grass, Cortaderia selloana, which is limited to zone 7 or 8 in most regions), making it the choice for gardeners in zones 6–8 who want the pampas grass effect. Thrives in hot, dry, poor conditions. Can self-seed in some regions — monitor and remove unwanted seedlings.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; eastern gamagrass tolerates periodic flooding, wet soil, and partial shade better than most ornamental grasses — excellent for wet rain garden margins and transitional areas.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to wet, average to fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; eastern gamagrass is a large, stately native North American grass that was once a dominant component of the tallgrass prairie and is now increasingly recognized as an ornamental plant for large-scale naturalistic gardens, rain gardens, and ecological restoration plantings. Its bold, coarse, corn-like foliage rises to 4–6 feet and turns a rich purple-bronze in fall before the leaves persist through winter. It is one of the most moisture-tolerant native grasses, thriving beside ponds, streams, and in wet meadows where most prairie grasses would fail, and tolerates partial shade better than most ornamental grasses. The unusual seed heads — resembling corn cobs in miniature — are botanically interesting and provide food for birds. A close relative of corn (Zea mays), gamagrass is the only North American relative of the corn genus and has been studied for potential breeding to improve corn's drought and flooding tolerance. Excellent for large rain gardens, bioswales, and wet native plant meadows.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, average soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; bushy bluestem naturally occurs in moist to wet coastal plain habitats and tolerates both periodic flooding and partial shade — making it one of the most adaptable native grasses for difficult, wet, partially shaded conditions.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to wet, average soil in full sun to partial shade; bushy bluestem is a southeastern US native grass that provides one of the finest and most underappreciated autumn and winter landscape effects of any native grass — from late summer through winter, the tips of the upright stems become densely bushy with clusters of fluffy, silvery-white, cottony seed heads that contrast brilliantly with the stems and foliage as they turn copper-bronze to bright orange-red in fall. The dense, terminal, feathery clusters give the plant its common name and make it look as though each stem has been tipped with a silver-white fluffy ball. It grows naturally in moist coastal plain flatwoods, roadside ditches, stream banks, and wet meadow margins, and is one of the best native grasses for these wet, difficult conditions. Tolerates partial shade and periodic flooding significantly better than most native grasses. Outstanding for autumn and winter wildlife (seed heads feed finches and sparrows) and naturalistic rain gardens in the Gulf South and eastern US.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist to wet, neutral to slightly saline, average to poor soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; salt meadow cordgrass is one of the most salt-tolerant ornamental grasses and can tolerate periodic inundation with brackish water — essential for coastal restoration and salt marsh edge plantings.

directions: Plant divisions in moist to wet, neutral to slightly saline soil in full sun to partial shade; salt meadow cordgrass (marsh hay cordgrass) is a native grass of coastal salt marshes, barrier island meadows, and estuarine edges from eastern Canada to the Gulf of Mexico that forms graceful, fine-textured, wispy, arching mounds of narrow, wiry, medium-green to golden-green leaves that wave and rustle attractively in coastal breezes, turning golden-tan in autumn. It is one of the most salt-tolerant native grasses and is essential for coastal restoration work, stabilizing salt marsh edges, protecting coastal shorelines from erosion, and naturalizing the transitional zone between dry upland and tidal marsh. The plant is extremely valuable in the ecology of coastal systems — it is a primary nesting habitat for Nelson's sparrows, saltmarsh sparrows, and other coastal specialty birds, and its dense root systems trap sediment and build marsh soil. For ornamental use, it creates a fine-textured, naturalistic groundcover in wet coastal gardens where few grasses perform.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
